title: Helpful links
text:
One of the great things about using R is the global community of R users and developers who collectively design and share a wealth of learning materials freely online. I’ve listed some of my favorites below.

Getting started with R
RYouWithMe is a great starter tutorial from RLadiesSydney and much better than anything I could put together. See more here
Swirl is an R package that you can install and then use to work your way through interactive tutorials. See more here.
Posit Recipes are code snippets for doing all kinds of tasks with R. See more here.
Many Cheatsheets have been created to help with various tasks in R. See more here.
If you want an entire book style introduction, there are few better than R for Data Science.


Style guides
As you develop R scripts, it helps to use a consistent style that will make them easier to read and easier to spot errors. It doesn’t particularly matter what style you use, just as long as you are consistent. Here are some example style guides:
Google’s R Style Guide
Tidyverse Style Guide

title: Deploying to the web
text:
Deploying to the web
I have set up a Netlify account and registered the your-own-statistician.com domain.
The website is created by clicking render, which builds all the files in the _site folder.
All the code is on github https://github.com/DarrenDahly/yos_quarto_web
When the _site folder is updated, commit and push the changes to github.
Netlify monitors the _site folder on github, and pushes it from there out to the web.
Change code -> Render site -> Push to github


Github set up
https://github.com/DarrenDahly/yos_quarto_web https://stackoverflow.com/questions/39435240/rstudio-changing-origin-for-git-version-control-of-project git remote add origin https://github.com/DarrenDahly/yos_quarto_web
git branch - M main
git push -u origin main
https://jennybc.github.io/2014-05-12-ubc/ubc-r/session03_git.html https://psiweb.org/docs/default-source/sig—aims/a-guide-to-using-git-and-github-through-r-studio-201910.pdf?sfvrsn=c5a7db_0
Netlify:
https://app.netlify.com/projects/delicate-yeot-fde6bf/deploys/6a216f55f56dfc04d0338e52
log in witi github darrendl1@gmail.com

title: Your Own Statistician
text:
Everything that follows is based on a simple set of premises.

Answers to important research questions in medicine and health often rely on quantitative data.
Designing studies to generate useful quantitative data requires expertise. The same goes for analyzing the data and interpreting the results.
Medical statisticians often have the expertise to contribute to the design, analysis and interpretation of quantitative data, but there are very few of them around.
This means that many or most researchers working in medicine and health have to serve as their own statistician, whether they realize it or not.
Failure to take this responsibility seriously can lead to otherwise avoidable research waste, as flaws in study design, analysis and interpretation can render research results useless, or even harmful.
Primum non nocere, or GTFO


Your-Own-Statistician is an informal, weekly, online meet-up aimed at supporting post-graduate students in the College of Medicine and Health at University College Cork to develop and maintain their quantitative data skills.
Meet-ups will be on Friday mornings at 10:30-11:30 via Teams (and occasionally in person). You are welcome to attend as you are able. You do not need to RSVP, and you do not need to let me know when you can’t make it :)
General topics will include:

Statistical and causal inference
Study design and data analysis planning
Evaluation of clinical prediction models/algorithms
Reproducible statistical programming with R and RStudio
Data visualisation
Data management and stewardship

The meet-up format will rotate through:

Training seminars/tutorials
Data analysis “show-and-tells”
Open Q&A and troubleshooting sessions
Article discussions

This website will be used to share materials.

title: Why R?
text:
R is a programming language designed to support the statistical analysis of data. It has several distinct advantages over commonly used statistical software, as well as some costs, all of which we will discuss.
I started my own research career using Stata and still hold it in high esteem. Eventually though I ran across a method for analyzing data that was only possible with R, so I took the plunge. It was hard at first. New things always are. But I powered through and soon came to realize the power of R as an open-source programming language that pretty much allowed me to do anything I wanted with research data; and to do it more efficiently, with fewer errors.
The challenges of using R have greatly diminished over time, largely due to a vast, dedicated community of R users and developers, and now I have no problems recommending it even for novice data analysts. Further, many now use R via a “integrated development environment” (a fancy term for interface) called RStudio, which makes using R even easier, as well as more powerful.
Critically, the tutorials and analysis examples we share in the meet-ups will be done using R and RStudio, so for that reason alone it would be helpful for those who haven’t used R to go ahead and take the plunge now :)
To get started, all you need to do is…
Install the appropriate version of R for your machine: https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/ (saying yes to the default set up suggestions unless you know better)
Install the appropriate version of RStudio IDE: https://docs.posit.co/ide/user/#rstudio-ide-oss-downloads (saying yes to the default set up suggestions unless you know better)
And if you can’t install these on your machine, or don’t want to, you can do pretty much anything in the cloud using a free account: https://posit.cloud/
Once you have R and RStudio installed, have a look at this introduction to the RStudio interface from RLadiesSydney

title: First steps
text:
In this session we will introduce the most fundamental aspects of using R, and at the end of the session you should feel more comfortable engaging with the many online resources that are out there to help people learn R to analyse data.
You should come away from this tutorial with a general sense of meaning for the following terms:
data types - data structures - objects - functions - arguments - object classes - assignment - operators - dataframes - pipes - packages - CRAN

title: First steps
text:
As aside on assignment
Other programming languages use = as an assignment operator and technically you can use it in R as well, though people have written many words about why you shouldn’t.

  x = c(1, 2, 3)      
  x

[1] 1 2 3


The assignment operator <- can also be used as -> though you will only rarely see this in practice.

  c(1, 2, 3) -> x      
  x

[1] 1 2 3

title: First steps
text:
Footnotes


For those coming from other programming languages, there are technically no scalars in R, just vectors of length 1. But I’m not a programmer and have no idea why anyone would care about this, so will leave it at that :)↩︎
There are also complex and raw types, but I’ve never used either and don’t suspect you will need to either.↩︎
